metadata.title: Why a turn budget stops a runaway agent more cleanly than a stopwatch
metadata.topic: Turn budgets as circuit breakers for agentic loops
metadata.purpose: Each request→response→tool cycle is one turn; the loop halts *after* a response, never mid-tool — so a turn budget always cuts at a semantically coherent boundary, while a clock fires at an arbitrary instant.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: agentic loop concept, GitHub Actions basics
metadata.exclusions: token-level budgets; cost-per-turn economics; multi-agent orchestration
metadata.visual_object: A progress bar filling one segment per turn (1→5→stop), beside a clock slicing the same bar at a random point mid-segment.
metadata.hook: A goal-seeking agent runs until the task is "done" — inside a CI job that must terminate, something has to cut it off.

beats.narration_text: With `max_turns: 5`, Claude reads files (turn 1), writes changes (turns 2–4), returns a summary (turn 5); the workflow exits with bounded, committed work. A 45-second wall-clock timeout instead fires mid-file-write, leaving a half-written file.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: A loop is open-ended; a CI job must end predictably. Why does a turn counter give a cleaner stopping condition than a wall-clock timeout?
A goal-seeking agent runs until the task is "done" — inside a CI job that must terminate, something has to cut it off.

Each request→response→tool cycle is one turn; the loop halts *after* a response, never mid-tool — so a turn budget always cuts at a semantically coherent boundary, while a clock fires at an arbitrary instant.
beats.visual: A progress bar filling one segment per turn (1→5→stop), beside a clock slicing the same bar at a random point mid-segment.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: 3-turn budget: read (1), patch (2), return diff (3) — exhausted cleanly. Same task, 45s timeout → fires during turn 2's write → corrupt file.

Each request→response→tool cycle is one turn; the loop halts *after* a response, never mid-tool — so a turn budget always cuts at a semantically coherent boundary, while a clock fires at an arbitrary instant.
